text: Code refactoring is the process of improving the internal structure of existing code without changing its external behavior. The main goal is to make the code cleaner, easier to read, and simpler to maintain. For example, you might rename confusing variables, break long functions into shorter ones, or remove duplicate code. Refactoring does not add new features—it focuses on reducing "technical debt," which is the cost of fixing messy or inefficient code later. A clean codebase saves time for developers and helps prevent bugs. Regular refactoring keeps software healthy, just like tidying up a room makes it more pleasant to work in.
